items.title: Anthropic gives 12 partners early access to Claude Mythos Preview to find zero-days before attackers do
items.summary: Project Glasswing — announced April 7 and gaining momentum through May — gives AWS, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorgan Chase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Palo Alto Networks early access to Claude Mythos Preview, an unreleased Anthropic frontier model that's strikingly good at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities. Mythos has already autonomously identified thousands of zero-days across every major OS and browser, including a 17-year-old FreeBSD remote-code-execution flaw (CVE-2026-4747) that gives root from an unauthenticated NFS request, and chained four vulns to escape a browser's renderer and OS sandboxes. Anthropic's own red.anthropic.com Mythos page now characterizes the model as a 'general-purpose language model that performs strongly across the board, but is strikingly capable at computer security tasks' — meaning when public release lands, the same capability ships everywhere. CrowdStrike posted a founding-member companion piece walking through how it's using Mythos internally for vulnerability triage. Anthropic is committing $100M in Mythos credits and $4M in donations to open-source security orgs; access is also being extended to 40+ additional critical-infrastructure maintainers.
items.why_it_matters: This is the clearest public signal yet that frontier models have crossed the line into 'better at offensive security than almost any human.' The way Anthropic is handling it — give defenders early access, fund open-source security work, hold the model back from public release — is the practical version of the abstract 'AI safety' debates. Worth reading the Schneier post and Anthropic's own write-up to see the specific exploit chains; if you administer any FreeBSD/NFS box, CVE-2026-4747 is the one to patch this week. The 'strikingly capable at computer security tasks' framing on Anthropic's own red-team site is the part to track — when Mythos becomes generally available, the offensive capabilities go with it.

revisions.change: added Anthropic red-team site framing of Mythos as general-purpose model strikingly capable at security tasks; added CrowdStrike founding-member writeup detail

takes.take: Calls Glasswing 'a useful experiment in selectively releasing dangerous capabilities' — defenders move first, then the model goes public.
Documents the scale of zero-day discovery — 'thousands' across major operating systems and browsers, with the FreeBSD NFS RCE singled out as the canary case.

Anthropic's own red-team page reframes Mythos as 'a general-purpose language model that performs strongly across the board, but is strikingly capable at computer security tasks' — i.e., the offensive capability is a property of a future general-release Claude, not a niche security tool.
Founding-member walkthrough of how CrowdStrike is integrating Mythos for vulnerability triage — concrete defender-side workflow, not just an endorsement quote.
Frames it as Anthropic's bet that defenders need a head start over the inevitable public-model parity moment.

items.title: Commerce's CAISI signs pre-deployment AI testing pacts with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI
items.summary: The Center for AI Standards and Innovation, sitting under NIST at the Department of Commerce, announced agreements on May 5–6 with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI to evaluate frontier models before they're publicly released. Each company will hand over its latest models — frequently with safeguards reduced or removed — for classified-environment testing of cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical-weapons risks, plus assessment of foreign AI systems for backdoors. The deals build on (and renegotiate) the original 2024 OpenAI and Anthropic agreements under the new America's AI Action Plan directives. CAISI now reports it has completed 40+ pre-deployment evaluations to date, including state-of-the-art models that have never been released publicly. Industry response has been notably uniform: BSA's Aaron Cooper credited CAISI with 'the necessary expertise to work with private sector partners,' and Microsoft's chief responsible AI officer Natasha Crampton called the deal a vehicle to 'advance the science of AI testing and evaluation' — the same language Microsoft used about the 2024 NIST AISI agreement.
items.why_it_matters: Government pre-deployment review used to be a voluntary OpenAI/Anthropic thing; it's now a five-lab club, including xAI, with explicit access to safeguard-stripped models. The CAISI evals are also where a lot of the public benchmark commentary on Chinese open-weight models (DeepSeek V4, Kimi K2.6) is coming from — same agency, same methodology. The 40+ completed evaluations stat is the scoreboard for how routine this has become. Worth tracking how this interacts with Anthropic's Pentagon exclusion: CAISI is doing safety evals at the same time DoD is freezing Anthropic out for being too cautious about safety.

revisions.change: added 40+ completed evaluations stat and industry-reaction quotes from BSA's Aaron Cooper and Microsoft's Natasha Crampton

takes.take: Reads the deals as the Trump admin extending the same pre-deployment-review template to a broader slate of labs.
Highlights the 'reduced or removed safeguards' provision as the most consequential clause for what kind of testing is actually possible.

Notes Microsoft's Natasha Crampton calling the agreement an effort to 'advance the science of AI testing and evaluation' — the exact phrasing used about the 2024 AISI deal, signaling continuity rather than reset.
Notes the agreements are flexible enough to expand to new model families without renegotiation.

items.title: Four Chinese labs ship open-weight coding models in 12 days, all clustered at the closed-frontier ceiling
items.summary: In a 12-day window in late April, Z.ai dropped GLM-5.1 (754B params, MIT) on April 7, Moonshot released Kimi K2.6 (1T params, Modified MIT) on April 20, MiniMax shipped M2.7 (10B activated), and DeepSeek shipped V4-Pro/V4-Flash (1.6T/284B, MIT) on April 24. New benchmark numbers tighten the picture: DeepSeek V4-Pro Max leads open-weights on SWE-Bench Verified at 80.6%, Kimi K2.6 sits at 80.2% (just 0.6 points behind Claude Opus 4.6 at 80.8%), and Kimi leads the four on SWE-Bench Pro at 58.6%. MiniMax M2.7 prices at $0.30/M input tokens and lands 56.22% on SWE-Bench Pro — about 94% of GLM-5.1's performance at roughly one-fifth the cost. Kimi K2.6 ships an Agent Swarm runtime with up to 300 sub-agents and 4,000 coordinated steps. NIST CAISI's evaluation of V4-Pro pegged it about 8 months behind the current frontier overall.
items.why_it_matters: Today's am file covered DeepSeek V4 in isolation — but the bigger pattern is that four labs hit the same capability ceiling within two weeks, all under permissive licenses, all at meaningfully lower inference cost than US closed models. The new SWE-Bench Verified spread (80.2% Kimi vs. 80.6% V4-Pro vs. 80.8% Opus 4.6) means 'is the Chinese open-weight at parity with closed US frontier on coding?' is now answered yes for SWE-Bench Verified — only Pro and per-task quality stay differentiated. If you're spinning up a Python project that defaults to an API, this is the moment when 'pull the weights and run them somewhere cheap' becomes a real default option for coding workloads. The MiniMax 94%-perf-at-20%-cost number is the price line every API vendor is benchmarking against now.

revisions.change: added tightened SWE-Bench Verified numbers (Kimi 80.2 / V4-Pro 80.6 / Opus 4.6 80.8) and SWE-Bench Pro leadership (Kimi 58.6, MiniMax 56.22 at $0.30/M)

takes.take: Pegs Kimi K2.6 at parity with Qwen3.6 Max and DeepSeek V4, just behind top closed models on agentic coding.
Side-by-side numbers across the four — SWE-Bench Verified, SWE-Bench Pro, and per-token pricing — with MiniMax M2.7 winning the price column at $0.30/M and Kimi K2.6 leading SWE-Bench Pro at 58.6%.
Independent benchmarking that puts V4-Pro Max at 80.6% on SWE-Bench Verified — the open-weight high-water mark, 0.2 points under Claude Opus 4.6.
Real-task tests on 15 coding problems: the 0.2-point SWE-Bench gap between Kimi K2.6 and GLM-5.1 hides a 43% price gap, and Kimi codes 11 points better in practice.

items.title: Hugging Face Transformers 5.8.0 lands with DeepSeek-V4, Granite Speech Plus, and Poolside's Laguna
items.summary: Transformers 5.8.0 shipped May 5 and adds first-class support for DeepSeek-V4 (Flash and Pro variants — the architecture replaces Multi-head Latent Attention with hybrid local + long-range attention, swaps residual connections for Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections, and bootstraps the first MoE layers with a static token-id → expert-id hash table), IBM Granite Speech Plus (audio transcription with speaker annotation and word-level timestamps), Granite Vision 4.1 (built on LLaVA-NeXT with SigLIP2 vision encoder, Window Q-Former projectors, and DeepStack feature injection across 8 vision-to-LLM injection points), and Laguna — Poolside's MoE language model with per-layer head counts (different decoder layers run different query-head counts while sharing KV-cache shape) and a sigmoid-router auxiliary-loss-free load-balancing scheme using element-wise sigmoid of gate logits plus learned per-expert bias. The library now claims 400+ supported architectures and over 750,000 model checkpoints on the Hub. Hugging Face also pushed huggingface-hub 1.14.0 the next day (May 6).
items.why_it_matters: If you write Python and load models from the Hub, this is the most direct path to running V4-Pro locally instead of via the API. The Laguna implementation is also worth a read just for the auxiliary-loss-free MoE balancing — it's an active research question that's now sitting in the Transformers source tree. Granite Vision 4.1's chart-and-table extraction is the kind of thing that quietly replaces a couple of bash + tesseract scripts. The DeepSeek-V4 'Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections' replacing residual connections is the one architectural change worth reading the paper for; it's a meaningful break from the post-norm/pre-norm transformer template most tutorials still teach.

revisions.change: added DeepSeek-V4 architecture details (mHC, hash-table-bootstrapped MoE), Granite Vision 4.1 build (LLaVA-NeXT + SigLIP2 + Window Q-Former + 8-point DeepStack), and Laguna sigmoid-router specifics; noted huggingface-hub 1.14.0 May 6 release

takes.take: Documents the per-release model cadence; the 5.8.0 cluster is the largest single-release additions in months.
Poolside's own writeup on Laguna XS.2 / M.1 — the SWE-Bench Verified scores (68.2% / 72.5%) and the engineering motivation for per-layer head counts.

items.title: Nvidia open-sources Ising — AI models built specifically for quantum-computer error correction and calibration
items.summary: Nvidia released the Ising family of open-source models at GTC and continued promoting them through May 5. Ising Calibration is a vision-language model that reads instrument measurements off quantum processors and replaces multi-day manual calibration runs with hour-scale automation. Ising Decoding ships two 3D-CNN variants (one tuned for speed, one for accuracy) for real-time quantum-error-correction decoding — Nvidia's benchmarks claim 2.5x faster and 3x more accurate than traditional decoders. Ising integrates with CUDA-Q and NVQLink and is hosted on Hugging Face, GitHub, and Nvidia's own platforms. Northwestern University and Fermilab were specifically deep collaborators on the training side, not just adopters: Fermilab's underground NEXUS facility generated the high-dimensional measurement datasets from custom superconducting devices that were used to train the Calibration model. Ising Calibration is now running on Fermilab's centralized GPU cluster behind the lab's model router, and is also in production at Harvard SEAS, IQM, LBNL's Advanced Quantum Testbed, and the UK's National Physical Laboratory.
items.why_it_matters: Quantum computing has had its own AI-tooling story going for a while; Ising is Nvidia making the bet that the path to fault-tolerant quantum runs through transformer-style models trained on calibration and decoding data. The interesting cross-stack idea: calibration is a vision-language model — it reads scope traces — and the decoder is a 3D CNN treating the syndrome graph as a volume. The Northwestern/Fermilab NEXUS detail is the part to skim — purpose-built physical experiment generates training data for a model that then ships back to operate the same class of hardware. It's the same training-loop pattern as RLHF, just with superconducting qubits instead of human raters.

revisions.change: added Northwestern + Fermilab NEXUS underground-facility training-data detail; clarified Fermilab integration via centralized GPU cluster + model router

takes.take: Frames the 2.5x/3x decoding numbers as a credible reset to the QEC-decoder timeline — vendors had been quoting late-decade horizons that Ising compresses.
The technical companion piece: how the calibration VLM reads measurement plots and the decoder treats syndrome data as a 3D volume.
Northwestern's own writeup of how its NEXUS-facility data fed into the Ising calibration training set — the experimental side of the loop.
Higher-ed angle on the launch adopters — Academia Sinica, Harvard SEAS, LBNL — and what classroom labs gain by skipping bespoke calibration code.

items.title: ServiceNow and Nvidia debut Project Arc — a self-evolving autonomous desktop agent with sandboxed runtime
items.summary: At ServiceNow Knowledge 2026 in Las Vegas on May 5, ServiceNow chair Bill McDermott and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang unveiled Project Arc, a long-running autonomous desktop agent for knowledge workers, developers, and IT teams that lives on the user's machine rather than in the cloud. Project Arc runs inside Nvidia OpenShell, a new open-source secure runtime for sandboxed, policy-governed agent execution, and connects to the ServiceNow AI Platform via Action Fabric so every command, file read, and workflow step is auditable through ServiceNow's AI Control Tower. The Control Tower + Nvidia Enterprise AI Factory validated design is generally available; Project Arc itself is an early preview. Both companies pitched it explicitly as an enterprise alternative to Microsoft's OpenClaw — which Microsoft Defender now blocks at the policy layer.
items.why_it_matters: Sierra's $15B agent fundraise yesterday and Microsoft Agent 365's GA earlier this month both pointed at the same race: who runs the governance and audit layer for autonomous desktop agents. Project Arc adds Nvidia + ServiceNow to that field, and the open-sourced OpenShell sandbox is the part worth a look even if you'll never deploy ServiceNow — it's a reference implementation of the policy-governed-runtime idea every Python or Tauri builder is going to need when their agent starts running shell commands. The framing as 'OpenClaw alternative' is also the cleanest signal yet that Microsoft Defender's OpenClaw block has cleared the way for non-Microsoft enterprise agents to be the obvious choice.

takes.take: ServiceNow's own framing positions Project Arc as governance extending from desktop to data center — same control plane, two deployment surfaces.

Reads the Knowledge 2026 keynote as ServiceNow's pitch for an 'autonomous workforce' that 'senses, decides, and securely acts' — the security-first framing aimed directly at the OpenClaw/Defender controversy.
Calls Project Arc 'an autonomous AI agent that lives on your work desktop' and emphasizes the AI Control Tower as the governance differentiator vs. consumer-grade desktop agents.

items.title: Bret Taylor's Sierra raises $950M at $15B valuation as agent-platform consolidation accelerates
items.summary: Sierra Technologies — Bret Taylor's customer-service agent platform, co-founded with former Google exec Clay Bavor in early 2024 — closed a $950M round at a roughly $15B post-money valuation on May 4 (some reporting calls it $15.8B), led by Alphabet's GV and Tiger Global, with Benchmark, Sequoia, and Greenoaks among others. Sierra topped $150M annual recurring revenue inside eight quarters from launch — a pace Taylor characterized as 'intense demand in the market' — and reports adoption by nearly half the Fortune 50. The valuation jumped from $10B in fall 2025 to ~$15B now. Taylor remains chair of OpenAI's board; the fundraise lands during the same week OpenAI and Anthropic both announced enterprise-services joint ventures, and the parallel Salesforce / Microsoft Agent 365 / Google Workspace Intelligence pushes are all dated within the past two weeks.
items.why_it_matters: $150M ARR for a two-year-old agent company in eight quarters explains why every major SaaS vendor — Salesforce, Microsoft, Google — is racing to ship its own agent platform. The valuation multiple (~100x ARR) is the data point that's reshaping what 'reasonable AI startup pricing' looks like for late-stage rounds. Bret Taylor wearing both the Sierra-CEO and OpenAI-board-chair hats while OpenAI launches its own agent product is the conflict-of-interest setup worth watching — none of the new reporting flags concrete governance changes, but the optics keep getting more strained as OpenAI's Development Company joint venture spins up.

revisions.change: added '$150M ARR in eight quarters' framing, valuation trajectory ($10B → ~$15B in two quarters), and Bret Taylor's 'intense demand' quote; noted parallel agent-platform announcements from Salesforce, Microsoft, Google

takes.take: Frames the round as 'the race to own enterprise AI' getting serious — Sierra is the pure-play comp every SaaS vendor is being measured against.
Notes the GV + Tiger Global lead and the Fortune-50 adoption stat as the case for the multiple.
Pegs the post-money at $15.8B (vs. CNBC's '$15B+'), which is the higher of the two figures circulating — useful when comparing against Microsoft/Salesforce internal-comp leaks.

items.title: ElevenLabs crosses $500M ARR, lists BlackRock, Nvidia, Salesforce Ventures, and Jamie Foxx in Series D close
items.summary: ElevenLabs disclosed on May 5–6 that its $500M Series D — first announced in February at an $11B valuation — closed with a roster including BlackRock, Wellington Management, NVIDIA's NVentures, Santander, D.E. Shaw, Schroders, Salesforce Ventures, Deutsche Telekom, plus celebrity backers Jamie Foxx, Eva Longoria, Squid Game creator Hwang Dong-hyuk, and existing investor Matthew McConaughey. ARR jumped from $350M at end-2025 to $500M+ in Q1 2026, driven by enterprise voice-agent deployments in support, sales, hiring, and marketing. Wellington's Rob Mazzoni called natural-voice infrastructure 'critical global infrastructure' as enterprises move to agent-first customer interaction; Deutsche Telekom T.Capital's Karine Peters framed the deal as ElevenLabs becoming foundational to DT's industrial-AI strategy. The company also closed a $100M employee tender — its second in under a year.
items.why_it_matters: Voice was the area most analysts wrote off as a rounding error a year ago; ElevenLabs is now at half a billion ARR with mainstream-finance investors and a celebrity index. The $350M-to-$500M+ jump in a single quarter is the data point arguing voice-agent revenue is compounding faster than chat-app revenue, not the other way around. The interesting detail is how much of that revenue is enterprise voice-agent infrastructure rather than creator/consumer use — that's the shift that justifies BlackRock, Wellington, and Schroders showing up. If you've been ignoring the voice stack for Python projects, this is the data point arguing it's now production-grade enough to wire in.

revisions.change: added expanded investor list (Wellington, Santander, Schroders, McConaughey), explicit ARR trajectory $350M → $500M+, and Wellington/T.Capital VC quotes characterizing the strategic case

takes.take: Reads the ARR jump as evidence that voice-agent deployment is the dominant growth lever, not consumer usage.
Captures Wellington's Rob Mazzoni quote on natural-voice infrastructure becoming 'critical global infrastructure' — institutional-investor logic for the round.
European angle on what the Deutsche Telekom + BlackRock combination signals for the EU voice market specifically.

items.title: Class action accuses OpenAI of routing ChatGPT queries to Meta and Google via Pixel + Analytics
items.summary: A proposed class action filed in the Northern District of California on May 5 alleges OpenAI embedded Meta's Facebook Pixel and Google Analytics tracking on the ChatGPT website, automatically transmitting users' queries and identifying information to both companies without consent. It's one of the first federal-court suits specifically alleging AI-chatbot user-data disclosure to ad/analytics platforms, and lands on the same Northern District docket already hosting Perplexity's similar class action filed in early April. The complaint leans on the Wiretap Act and California's CIPA. Independent academic backing landed at the same time: researchers at Spain's IMDEA Networks Institute reported 13+ third-party trackers across ChatGPT, Claude, Grok, and Perplexity sending conversation URLs and user data to Meta, Google, and TikTok — the empirical core of the lawsuit's factual theory.
items.why_it_matters: If you've been pasting work or personal stuff into ChatGPT through the web app, this complaint is alleging that the visible text — plus identifying cookies — got sent to Meta and Google's analytics pipelines. The IMDEA study turning up the same pattern across Claude, Grok, and Perplexity means it's not OpenAI-specific, and it's not just the lawyers spinning a theory. Whether or not the complaint survives a motion to dismiss, the allegation will pressure every AI chat product to audit and document its embedded trackers. For Python web devs, it's a concrete reminder that 'I just dropped in Pixel and GA' is increasingly load-bearing legal exposure on any product that takes user free-text input.

revisions.change: added IMDEA Networks Institute study finding 13+ trackers across ChatGPT/Claude/Grok/Perplexity, providing academic support for the lawsuit's factual theory

takes.take: Walks through the specific claims — Pixel and GA scripts on the ChatGPT site automatically firing on every query — and the Wiretap Act / CIPA theories.
Summarizes the IMDEA Networks Institute findings on chatbot tracker leakage — the same pattern the OpenAI complaint alleges, replicated across four major chat products.
Notes the parallel Perplexity suit from early April, which establishes the venue and the wiretap-statute theory the OpenAI complaint is borrowing.

items.title: Connecticut passes SB 5, the seventh state chatbot-safety law and one of the broader AI bills in the US
items.summary: Connecticut's Senate Bill 5 cleared both chambers on May 5 (House 131–17, Senate 32–4), and Governor Ned Lamont — who had previously opposed broad AI regulation — said he'll sign it. The 67-page law bundles several AI bills into one package. The chatbot-safety section, effective January 1, 2027, defines 'AI companion' broadly as any AI that 'communicates with individuals in natural language' and 'simulates human conversation and interaction through text, audio or video,' and requires providers to detect expressions of suicide, self-harm, or imminent violence and refer users to mental-health resources, plus display an 'I am not human' notice initially and every hour. A separate section, effective October 1, 2026, amends Connecticut's employment-discrimination statute to cover AI employment-decision tools (AEDPs). Connecticut joins New York, California, Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and Iowa (whose SF 2417 was signed by Gov. Reynolds on May 2) as states with conversational-AI safety laws.
items.why_it_matters: If you ever spin up a Python chatbot using the OpenAI or Claude API, the patchwork of state laws is now real — Connecticut is the seventh state, and the 'AI companion' definition is broad enough that almost any free-text natural-language interface qualifies. The most concrete spec to internalize: the every-hour 'this is a bot' reminder is now law in CT starting Jan 1 2027, and detection-and-referral for self-harm content is a service-level requirement, not just a moderation nice-to-have. The AEDP employment-tool clause is the other half worth tracking — applies to any HR product that scores or screens candidates, not just chatbots.

revisions.change: created (late-breaking pass — Connecticut SB 5 floor passage)

takes.take: Practitioner-side breakdown of the AI-companion definition and the hourly-disclosure requirement; flags it as the broadest US 'AI companion' definition to date.
Frames SB 5 as the most comprehensive state AI bill yet and notes Lamont's reversal — the political angle on why this passed now after years of stalling.
Roll-up of the seven-state chatbot-law cluster — Connecticut, NY, CA, WA, OR, ID, IA — with the per-state diffs in scope, exemptions, and enforcement.
